channel.title: The Daily Plant
channel.description: An automatically generated daily podcast for the serious gardener and designer. Each episode is a focused, master-gardener-level deep dive on a single plant or genus relevant to the Eastern US — native species, the non-native ornamentals and invasives you encounter in real gardens — covering identification, range and ecology, culture, mature habit, design use, companions, and the best cultivars. Most episodes end with a short spaced-repetition quiz on plants covered earlier, to build lasting recall.
channel.itunes:subtitle: A daily, expert-level deep dive on one plant or genus for East Coast garden designers.
channel.itunes:summary: An automatically generated daily podcast for the serious gardener and designer. Each episode is a focused, master-gardener-level deep dive on a single plant or genus relevant to the Eastern US — native species, the non-native ornamentals and invasives you encounter in real gardens — covering identification, range and ecology, culture, mature habit, design use, companions, and the best cultivars. Most episodes end with a short spaced-repetition quiz on plants covered earlier, to build lasting recall.
channel.itunes:author: Andrew Merluzzi

item.title: The Daily Plant — Native Deciduous Azaleas (Rhododendron)
item.description:
The eastern native deciduous azaleas are one of the most underused plant groups in East Coast garden design — a genus with bloom succession running from March to August, extraordinary fragrance variation, and tight ecological relationships unavailable from any hybrid. This episode covers the seven key species a designer needs to know, their cultural requirements and failure modes in real East Coast conditions, the specialist bee and sphinx moth that depend on them, and how to think about the native species versus the hybrid trade stock.
In this episode: • Our Eastern Native Azaleas in the Twenty-first Century — • Rhododendron periclymenoides (Pinxterbloom Azalea) — • Rhododendron calendulaceum (Flame Azalea) — • Rhododendron atlanticum (Coastal Azalea) — • Rhododendron (genus overview) — • Pinxterbloom Azalea (Rhododendron periclymenoides) — • Flame Azalea (Rhododendron calendulaceum) — • Sweet Azalea (Rhododendron arborescens) — • Piedmont Azalea (Rhododendron canescens) — • Native Azaleas — • Planting Native Azaleas Successfully in Clay Soil — • Pink Azalea: Native Shrub for Pollinators — • Azalea Sphinx (Darapsa choerilus) — • Azaleas and Home Gardens —
item.itunes:summary:
The eastern native deciduous azaleas are one of the most underused plant groups in East Coast garden design — a genus with bloom succession running from March to August, extraordinary fragrance variation, and tight ecological relationships unavailable from any hybrid. This episode covers the seven key species a designer needs to know, their cultural requirements and failure modes in real East Coast conditions, the specialist bee and sphinx moth that depend on them, and how to think about the native species versus the hybrid trade stock.
In this episode: • Our Eastern Native Azaleas in the Twenty-first Century — • Rhododendron periclymenoides (Pinxterbloom Azalea) — • Rhododendron calendulaceum (Flame Azalea) — • Rhododendron atlanticum (Coastal Azalea) — • Rhododendron (genus overview) — • Pinxterbloom Azalea (Rhododendron periclymenoides) — • Flame Azalea (Rhododendron calendulaceum) — • Sweet Azalea (Rhododendron arborescens) — • Piedmont Azalea (Rhododendron canescens) — • Native Azaleas — • Planting Native Azaleas Successfully in Clay Soil — • Pink Azalea: Native Shrub for Pollinators — • Azalea Sphinx (Darapsa choerilus) — • Azaleas and Home Gardens —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Foamflower (Tiarella)
item.description:
Tiarella, the foamflowers, are among the most functionally valuable native perennials for the East Coast woodland garden — yet they're too often treated as filler. This episode covers the full genus: the 2021 taxonomic revision that split the old Tiarella cordifolia complex into five distinct species, the critical design distinction between stoloniferous and clumping types, culture and heat-humidity performance across our climate zone, and key cultivar lines from Terra Nova and Primrose Path Nurseries. Closes with a spaced-repetition quiz on the Aesculus genus.
In this episode: • Tiarella cordifolia (False Miterwort, Foamflower) — • New Species of Foamflower — • Tiptoeing Through the Tiarella — • Foamflower — • Tiarella cordifolia — Plant Finder — • Tiarella — An Introduction to Foam Flowers — • Foamflower — • x Heucherella (Foamy Bells) — • Tiarella cordifolia —
item.itunes:summary:
Tiarella, the foamflowers, are among the most functionally valuable native perennials for the East Coast woodland garden — yet they're too often treated as filler. This episode covers the full genus: the 2021 taxonomic revision that split the old Tiarella cordifolia complex into five distinct species, the critical design distinction between stoloniferous and clumping types, culture and heat-humidity performance across our climate zone, and key cultivar lines from Terra Nova and Primrose Path Nurseries. Closes with a spaced-repetition quiz on the Aesculus genus.
In this episode: • Tiarella cordifolia (False Miterwort, Foamflower) — • New Species of Foamflower — • Tiptoeing Through the Tiarella — • Foamflower — • Tiarella cordifolia — Plant Finder — • Tiarella — An Introduction to Foam Flowers — • Foamflower — • x Heucherella (Foamy Bells) — • Tiarella cordifolia —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Buckeyes (Aesculus)
item.description:
A genus-level deep dive into Aesculus — the buckeyes — with an East Coast designer's lens. The episode covers all four native East Coast species, the two non-natives you routinely encounter, ecological roles from ruby-throated hummingbirds to giant silk moths, and the one understory shrub that belongs in every shade garden specification.
In this episode: • Aesculus parviflora (Bottlebrush Buckeye) — • Aesculus pavia (Red Buckeye) — • Aesculus flava (Yellow Buckeye) — • Aesculus sylvatica (Painted Buckeye) — • Aesculus parviflora — Plant Finder — • Aesculus pavia — Plant Finder — • How to Grow and Care for Bottlebrush Buckeye — • The Other Bottlebrush Buckeye: 'Rogers' Strain — • Red Buckeye (Aesculus pavia) — • Aesculus pavia (Red Buckeye) — • Buckeye? Horse Chestnut? Which One is Which? — • Painted Buckeye (Aesculus sylvatica) —
item.itunes:summary:
A genus-level deep dive into Aesculus — the buckeyes — with an East Coast designer's lens. The episode covers all four native East Coast species, the two non-natives you routinely encounter, ecological roles from ruby-throated hummingbirds to giant silk moths, and the one understory shrub that belongs in every shade garden specification.
In this episode: • Aesculus parviflora (Bottlebrush Buckeye) — • Aesculus pavia (Red Buckeye) — • Aesculus flava (Yellow Buckeye) — • Aesculus sylvatica (Painted Buckeye) — • Aesculus parviflora — Plant Finder — • Aesculus pavia — Plant Finder — • How to Grow and Care for Bottlebrush Buckeye — • The Other Bottlebrush Buckeye: 'Rogers' Strain — • Red Buckeye (Aesculus pavia) — • Aesculus pavia (Red Buckeye) — • Buckeye? Horse Chestnut? Which One is Which? — • Painted Buckeye (Aesculus sylvatica) —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Bluestar (Amsonia)
item.description:
A genus-level deep dive into the three Amsonia species that define East Coast garden practice: Amsonia tabernaemontana (the genuine East Coast native), Amsonia ciliata (southeastern dry-site specialist), and Amsonia hubrichtii (Ouachita native, widely planted outside its range). Covers native range caveats, site-specific culture, ecological roles as larval hosts for sphinx moths, and Mount Cuba Center trial top performers including the cultivars 'Spring Sky' and 'Short Stack.' Closes with a Kalmia latifolia spaced-repetition quiz.
In this episode: • Amsonia for the Mid-Atlantic Region — • Amsonia tabernaemontana — Eastern Bluestar — • Amsonia hubrichtii — Hubricht's Bluestar — • Native Amsonia for the Home Garden — • Amsonia tabernaemontana — Plant Finder — • Amsonia ciliata — Fringed Bluestar — • Mt. Cuba Center Trial of Amsonia Plants —
item.itunes:summary:
A genus-level deep dive into the three Amsonia species that define East Coast garden practice: Amsonia tabernaemontana (the genuine East Coast native), Amsonia ciliata (southeastern dry-site specialist), and Amsonia hubrichtii (Ouachita native, widely planted outside its range). Covers native range caveats, site-specific culture, ecological roles as larval hosts for sphinx moths, and Mount Cuba Center trial top performers including the cultivars 'Spring Sky' and 'Short Stack.' Closes with a Kalmia latifolia spaced-repetition quiz.
In this episode: • Amsonia for the Mid-Atlantic Region — • Amsonia tabernaemontana — Eastern Bluestar — • Amsonia hubrichtii — Hubricht's Bluestar — • Native Amsonia for the Home Garden — • Amsonia tabernaemontana — Plant Finder — • Amsonia ciliata — Fringed Bluestar — • Mt. Cuba Center Trial of Amsonia Plants —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Mountain Laurel (Kalmia latifolia)
item.description:
Kalmia latifolia — mountain laurel — is at peak bloom right now across the East Coast, and this episode gives it the full treatment: native range, the mechanical ingenuity of its catapult pollination system, the soil chemistry requirements that determine success or failure in cultivation, the Richard Jaynes breeding program at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station, and a cultivar guide organized for practical design use. Closes with a spaced-repetition quiz on Cornus florida.
In this episode: • Kalmia latifolia (Mountain Laurel) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Kalmia latifolia — USDA Forest Service Fire Effects Information System — • Kalmia latifolia — Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — • Kalmia latifolia — UConn Plant Database — • Mountain Laurel: A Plant with Many Faces — JARS v52n3 — • Kalmia latifolia — Go Botany, Native Plant Trust — • Kalmia latifolia — Native Gardens of Blue Hill — • Sphinx kalmiae (Laurel Sphinx) — Butterflies and Moths of North America — • Grayanotoxin Poisoning: Mad Honey Disease and Beyond — PMC —
item.itunes:summary:
Kalmia latifolia — mountain laurel — is at peak bloom right now across the East Coast, and this episode gives it the full treatment: native range, the mechanical ingenuity of its catapult pollination system, the soil chemistry requirements that determine success or failure in cultivation, the Richard Jaynes breeding program at the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station, and a cultivar guide organized for practical design use. Closes with a spaced-repetition quiz on Cornus florida.
In this episode: • Kalmia latifolia (Mountain Laurel) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Kalmia latifolia — USDA Forest Service Fire Effects Information System — • Kalmia latifolia — Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — • Kalmia latifolia — UConn Plant Database — • Mountain Laurel: A Plant with Many Faces — JARS v52n3 — • Kalmia latifolia — Go Botany, Native Plant Trust — • Kalmia latifolia — Native Gardens of Blue Hill — • Sphinx kalmiae (Laurel Sphinx) — Butterflies and Moths of North America — • Grayanotoxin Poisoning: Mad Honey Disease and Beyond — PMC —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Flowering Dogwood (Cornus florida)
item.description:
Cornus florida, the flowering dogwood, is the signature understory tree of the eastern North American forest and one of the most ecologically consequential natives a designer can specify. This episode covers diagnostic ID and common confusables, the East Coast culture requirements that determine success or failure, the Discula destructiva anthracnose epidemic and what it means for specification today, the cultivar Appalachian Spring and its unusual origin, specific Lepidoptera and specialist bee relationships, the high-fat fruit ecology tied to fall bird migration, design application in the understory layer, and an honest ecological accounting of hybrid dogwoods. Closes with expert-level Baptisia recall.
In this episode: • Cornus florida (Flowering Dogwood) — • Cornus florida f. rubra (Pink-Flowering Dogwood) — • Rutgers Dogwood Breeding History — • Dogwood Borer (Synanthedon scitula) Fact Sheet — • Flowering Dogwood (Cornus florida) — • UT Grows Disease-Resistant Dogwoods — • Dogwood Anthracnose — • Dogwood Anthracnose —
item.itunes:summary:
Cornus florida, the flowering dogwood, is the signature understory tree of the eastern North American forest and one of the most ecologically consequential natives a designer can specify. This episode covers diagnostic ID and common confusables, the East Coast culture requirements that determine success or failure, the Discula destructiva anthracnose epidemic and what it means for specification today, the cultivar Appalachian Spring and its unusual origin, specific Lepidoptera and specialist bee relationships, the high-fat fruit ecology tied to fall bird migration, design application in the understory layer, and an honest ecological accounting of hybrid dogwoods. Closes with expert-level Baptisia recall.
In this episode: • Cornus florida (Flowering Dogwood) — • Cornus florida f. rubra (Pink-Flowering Dogwood) — • Rutgers Dogwood Breeding History — • Dogwood Borer (Synanthedon scitula) Fact Sheet — • Flowering Dogwood (Cornus florida) — • UT Grows Disease-Resistant Dogwoods — • Dogwood Anthracnose — • Dogwood Anthracnose —

item.title: The Daily Plant — Wild Indigo (Baptisia)
item.description:
A genus-level deep dive on Baptisia, the native wild indigos — covering all five East Coast-relevant species, the critical culture notes most designers get wrong, the specialist pollinator and butterfly relationships (including the conservation-urgent Frosted Elfin), and a full survey of the major cultivar lineages with Mt. Cuba trial performance data. It is peak bloom right now on the East Coast, and this genus deserves a permanent place in your design palette.
In this episode: • Baptisia Trials — Mt. Cuba Center — • Baptisia (False Indigo, Wild Indigo) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Baptisia australis (Blue False Indigo) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Baptisia (False or Wild Indigo) — Clemson Home and Garden Information Center — • Baptisia australis — Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — • Frosted Elfin (Callophrys irus) — US Fish and Wildlife Service — • Wild Indigo Duskywing (Erynnis baptisiae) — Butterflies and Moths of North America —
item.itunes:summary:
A genus-level deep dive on Baptisia, the native wild indigos — covering all five East Coast-relevant species, the critical culture notes most designers get wrong, the specialist pollinator and butterfly relationships (including the conservation-urgent Frosted Elfin), and a full survey of the major cultivar lineages with Mt. Cuba trial performance data. It is peak bloom right now on the East Coast, and this genus deserves a permanent place in your design palette.
In this episode: • Baptisia Trials — Mt. Cuba Center — • Baptisia (False Indigo, Wild Indigo) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Baptisia australis (Blue False Indigo) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — • Baptisia (False or Wild Indigo) — Clemson Home and Garden Information Center — • Baptisia australis — Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — • Frosted Elfin (Callophrys irus) — US Fish and Wildlife Service — • Wild Indigo Duskywing (Erynnis baptisiae) — Butterflies and Moths of North America —
